question.text: An investor sets up an SWP of Rs. 15,000 per month from an equity mutual fund. The fund redeems units worth Rs. 15,000 each month. Which part of this withdrawal is subject to capital gains tax?

answer.text: The full Rs. 15,000 is taxable as capital gains
Only the profit (gain) portion of the redeemed units is taxable
The entire corpus value is taxable at the time of setting up SWP

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Each SWP withdrawal is treated as a redemption of units. Only the capital gain (profit) portion is taxable, not the full withdrawal amount. For example, if units worth Rs. 15,000 are redeemed and the cost of those specific units was Rs. 12,000, only Rs. 3,000 (the gain) is taxable. The remaining Rs. 12,000 is a return of the investor's own capital and is not taxed. **The Trap:** SWP is not completely tax-free, but only the gain portion is taxed. This makes SWP more tax-efficient than IDCW where the entire amount received is taxed at slab rate. The holding period of the specific units redeemed determines whether the gain is STCG or LTCG. **Cross-Exam Context:** Also tested in V-A. Important concept for retiree-focused questions. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Use this fact to demonstrate the tax advantage of SWP to retired investors. In early years of SWP, the gain portion of each withdrawal is typically small, making the tax impact minimal compared to IDCW.
